title: Refine Simple Route JSON Extraction to Separate Routing Intent from Existing PCB Trace State
description: Updates Simple Route JSON generation to ensure top-level autorouting inputs are derived from logical source_tracesource_net intent, rather than treating existing top-level pcb_trace records as already-routed state, addressing the dataset-srj18 missing traces issue.
body:
Updates Simple Route JSON generation so top-level autorouting inputs are built from logical `source_trace` / `source_net` intent instead of treating existing top-level `pcb_trace` records as already-routed state this will fix the dataset-srj18 missing traces issue.

This PR includes:

* Ignores existing top-level `pcb_trace` records when generating SRJ without a `subcircuit_id`
* Keeps existing routed-trace handling for explicit subcircuit routing contexts
* Preserves `externallyConnectedPointIds` behavior for subcircuit-aware routing
* Deduplicates net-derived PCB port connection points by `pcb_port_id`
* Adds regression coverage for both top-level and subcircuit SRJ behavior

This prevents already-routed board state, such as KiCad-imported copper, from suppressing fresh autorouting work while preserving the existing breakout/subcircuit routing semantics.

title: Fix custom symbol port direction for schematic netlabels
description: Fixes incorrect facing direction for custom symbol ports in schematics, ensuring netlabels point correctly based on declared port direction.
body:
Root cause:
Explicit custom-symbol ports with schX/schY were not using their declared `<port direction="...">` for `schematic_port.facing_direction`. Core instead inferred facing_direction from the port position relative to the parent component center. That could store `facing_direction: "left"` even when the custom symbol port explicitly declared `direction="right"`.

Netlabels use `schematic_port.facing_direction` to choose their `anchor_side`, so the incorrect facing direction caused fallback netlabels to point the wrong way.

Fix:
For explicit custom-symbol ports, core now uses `props.direction` as the source of truth for facingDirection. This keeps `direction`, `facing_direction`, and `side_of_component` consistent for custom symbol ports.

title: Document oval hole support
description: Updates the documentation for the hole  component to include support for oval holes, adding an example and updating the properties table accordingly.
body:
## Summary

This PR updates the `<hole />` docs to include `oval` as a supported hole shape.

## What Changed

- added `oval` to the supported shape list
- added an oval-hole example to the docs page
- updated the properties table so `shape`, `width`, and `height` reflect oval support
- renamed the rotation section to `Rotated Holes` while keeping rotation docs limited to the shapes currently supported in `core`

## Why

`../core` already supports plain `shape="oval"` for `Hole`, but the docs page still listed only circle, pill, and rect. This left the docs out of sync with the implementation.

## Validation

- reviewed `docs/elements/hole.mdx`
- checked `../core/lib/components/primitive-components/Hole.ts`
- checked `../core/tests/components/primitive-components/hole-oval.test.tsx`

## Notes

I intentionally did not document rotated oval holes because the current `core` implementation does not wire `pcbRotation` for `shape="oval"` yet.

title: Avoid shifting overlapped traces through schematic component boxes
description: Fixes the issue where overlapping traces would shift into schematic component boxes by implementing obstacle-aware offsets during trace separation.
body:
`TraceOverlapIssueSolver` used a fixed offset when separating overlapping traces. In this repro, that fixed shift moved the R2 trace into the resistor’s schematic box.

Now, the solver checks the shifted segment against schematic component obstacles. If the default offset would collide, it tries offsets based on the actual obstacle edges and picks the nearest clear one. This keeps the normal shift behavior when it is valid, and only adjusts the offset when it would route through a component.

title: Fix netlabel connector traces overlapping label edges
description: Fixes validation of connector traces to ensure they do not overlap netlabel edges, preventing potential routing errors.
body:
**Before**:
Connector traces were only rejected when they crossed through the interior of another netlabel. If a trace ran exactly along the label’s edge, it was treated as valid, and short connector traces could escape the final collision check.

**Now**:
Connector traces are validated against the full netlabel bounds, including edges. The final collision pass checks all trace segments, so edge overlaps are detected and rerouted away.

title: Fix parent SRJ conversion leaking descendant subcircuit nets
description: Fixes autorouting failure by ensuring parent SRJ generation does not include descendant subcircuit source nets, preventing duplicate route requests and static reachability errors.
body:
```md
## Summary

Fixes `getSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson` so parent subcircuit routing does not include descendant subcircuit `source_net`s as new route connections.

Previously, the converter used `relevantSubcircuitIds` when collecting `source_net`s. That set includes the current subcircuit plus all descendant subcircuits. This is correct for collecting obstacles and already-routed child traces, but incorrect for creating route connections. It caused parent autorouting SRJ to include inner subcircuit nets that were already routed.

## Problem

For boards with nested subcircuits, parent SRJ generation could produce both:

- existing child `pcb_trace`s in `traces`
- duplicate route requests for the child subcircuit's `source_net`s in `connections`

That made the autorouter try to route already-routed inner nets again, leading to static reachability failures.

## Fix

When collecting `source_net`s for `connectionsFromNets`, only include nets whose `subcircuit_id` matches the current `subcircuit_id`.

Descendant subcircuits are still included for obstacles and existing child traces, but their nets are no longer emitted as parent-level route requests.

## Verification

Tested against the subcircuit autorouting repro:

Before:

- `connections: 8`
- included `source_trace_34` plus descendant `source_net_0..source_net_6`
- autorouter failed with static reachability errors

After:

- `connections: 1`
- includes only `source_trace_34`
- keeps `traces: 21` existing child routes
- autorouter solves successfully
```

title: skip unplaceable net labels instead of failing the pipeline, keep straight pin-to-pin traces anchored
description: Fixes pipeline failure when a single net label cannot be placed, allowing the solver to continue processing other labels and preserving straight pin-to-pin traces.
body:
## Problem

  The pipeline failed entirely when a single net label couldn't be placed, even though all remaining traces and labels could have been solved (example37, example27, example40 all failed with
  "Could not place net label without collisions"). Additionally, the U1.1–C1.2 trace in example37 was rendered as a diagonal detached from its pin instead of a straight line.

  ## Changes

  ### NetLabelPlacementSolver: skip failed label groups
  When a `SingleNetLabelPlacementSolver` fails (after the existing all-orientations fallback), the group is recorded in `failedGroups` and the solver moves on to the next group instead of
  failing the whole pipeline. Mirrors the existing `failedConnectionPairs` pattern in `SchematicTraceLinesSolver`.

  ### TraceOverlapShiftSolver: keep straight pin-to-pin traces intact
  - `applyJogToTerminalSegment` mishandled 2-point traces: the only segment is both the first and last segment, so the `si === 0` branch jogged the start but shifted the end point off its
  pin. Both ends now jog inward, keeping both pin endpoints anchored.
  - When two nets overlap and one of them is a straight pin-to-pin trace, the straight trace now holds its position (offset 0) and only the other net's segment shifts. Previously both nets
  always shifted, forcing jogs onto an otherwise straight line.

  ### SingleOverlapSolver: reject reroutes that land on other traces
  Reroute candidates were only validated against chip obstacles, so a detour could end up exactly coincident with another net's trace (in example37 the detour clearance `0.2 + 0.1` landed
  precisely on the y=0.3 trace row). Candidates are now also rejected when any segment is parallel and coincident (within 2e-3) with a different-net trace; perpendicular crossings remain
  allowed. The full trace list is threaded down as `tracesToAvoidOverlapping` from `TraceLabelOverlapAvoidanceSolver` through `OverlapAvoidanceStepSolver`.

title: Prevent the untangle pass from routing traces through component bodies
description: Prevents the untangle pass from making valid traces invalid by rejecting reroute candidates that cross component bodies, ensuring valid paths are maintained during trace cleanup.
body:
Root cause
The trace arrives at the cleanup stage already valid — routed around C1 at x=-3.7, with one benign C1.1-U1.5 wire crossing:  incoming path:  (-2.7,1.1) → (-3.7,1.1) → (-3.7,-2.2) → (-4.7,-2.2) → (-4.7,-2.0)   ✓ clear of C1

Inside TraceCleanupSolver, the UntangleTraceSubsolver tries to remove that C1.1-U1.5 crossing. But it only evaluates reroute candidates against other traces, never against component bodies — and the only candidate it generates shifts the vertical run to x = -4.9, inside C1's box (x ∈ [-4.965, -4.435]):

untangle reroutes to:  (-2.7,1.1) → (-4.9,1.1) → (-4.9,-2.2) → (-4.7,-2.2) → (-4.7,-2.0)   ✗ through C1

So a stage that's supposed to clean things up takes a valid path and makes it invalid.

The fix
Add a guard in the untangle's candidate evaluation: a reroute candidate is rejected if any of its segments crosses a component, using the existing ChipObstacleSpatialIndex. (The candidate only covers the rerouted corner, never the pin-terminal segments, so the check is clean.)

With the guard, the only available reroute is rejected, so the untangle declines to reroute and keeps the incoming x=-3.7 path as-is — the stage either improves the layout or leaves an already-valid trace untouched, but never breaks it.

This mirrors the guard TraceOverlapShiftSolver already has, so both trace-shifting stages now prevent invalid states at the source rather than relying on a downstream correction pass.

title: Compact hop cost storage for port-to-region search
description: Reduces memory usage in TinyHyperGraphSolver by changing the indexing and storage of per-hop best-cost state during route search, optimizing for actual port connectivity instead of global region count.
body:
## Summary

This change reduces `TinyHyperGraphSolver` memory usage by changing how per-hop best-cost state is indexed and stored during route search.

Previously, dense candidate-cost arrays were allocated at `portCount * regionCount`, because hop ids were encoded directly from `(portId, nextRegionId)`. That forced the solver to reserve dense storage for every possible port/region pair, even though each port can only transition into a small subset of incident regions.

This patch replaces that layout with compact incident-slot indexing:

- dense hop-state arrays are sized at `portCount * hopSlotStride`
- `hopSlotStride` is the maximum incident-region count across all ports
- hops are keyed by `(portId, incident-region-slot)` for normal dense access
- non-incident hops fall back to a small overflow `Map`

The result is that dense hop bookkeeping scales with actual per-port connectivity instead of global region count, which removes the dominant source of memory blowup on large port-point-pathing hypergraphs.

title: Reduce solver memory pressure in duplicate-port and section-pipeline flows
description: Reduces memory usage across the duplicate-port repair path and the section pipeline, while fixing a regression around serialized metadata preservation.
body:
# PR Title

Reduce solver memory pressure in duplicate-port and section-pipeline flows

## Summary

This PR reduces memory usage across the duplicate-port repair path and the section pipeline, while fixing a regression around serialized metadata preservation.

## What changed

- Reworked `TinyHyperGraphSolver` candidate cost storage to use compact per-port incident-region slots instead of dense `portCount * regionCount` arrays.
- Added overflow handling for non-incident hops and explicit cleanup hooks to release transient search state after solving.
- Updated the section pipeline to reuse cached section-stage params, skip the optimize stage when the section mask is empty, and release no-longer-needed solver state after each stage.
- Reduced memory in `DuplicateCongestedPortSolver` by avoiding unnecessary deep cloning, stripping topology metadata before per-route solves, and precomputing serialized port IDs for reporting.
- Fixed serialized graph metadata handling so custom region/port metadata fields survive load/solve/output round-trips.
- Adjusted section replay scoring to rebuild solved solvers from solutions directly, so final congestion scoring reflects the actual solved output with the right solve options.

## Why

The recent duplicate-port and section-optimization flows were holding onto more solver/search state than necessary and allocating large dense structures that scale poorly with graph size. These changes keep the same behavior while lowering peak memory use and cleaning up state earlier. The metadata fix ensures the memory optimizations do not drop user-provided serialized fields.

title: Fix pcb_trace route handling for through_pad points in DSN session round-trips
description: Fixes a bug in DSN conversion that incorrectly handled through_pad points in pcb_trace routes, ensuring type safety and correct route processing.
body:
## Summary

This PR fixes a DSN conversion bug caused by `pcb_trace.route` now allowing `through_pad` points alongside `wire` and `via` points.

Previously, the DSN conversion pipeline assumed every route point had `x/y` coordinates. That broke type safety and could also lead to incorrect route processing when a `through_pad` point appeared in a trace route.

## What changed

- added explicit route-point narrowing for coordinate-bearing `pcb_trace` points
- updated circuit-json -> DSN conversion to avoid reading `x/y` from `through_pad` points
- updated DSN session -> circuit-json via reconstruction to only match coordinate-bearing route points
- preserved layer-change handling by starting a fresh wire segment when the previous route point is non-coordinate
- tightened the round-trip test typings to reflect the real `wire | via | through_pad` route union

## Bug reproduced and fixed

This PR includes the reproduction and the fix in the same change set:

- reproduced the issue in the DSN session round-trip path
- fixed the conversion logic so `through_pad` route points no longer get treated like `wire`/`via` points

## Why this matters

This is a core trace/data-model correctness fix in the DSN conversion pipeline:

- prevents invalid coordinate access on non-coordinate route points
- keeps DSN session round-trips compatible with the expanded `pcb_trace.route` model

title: Fix polygon SMT pad export producing NaN DSN padstack and pin coordinates
description: Fixes DSN export for pcb_smtpad elements with shape: polygon by ensuring proper polygon padstack generation and preventing NaN values in exported DSN output.
body:
## Summary

Fix DSN export for `pcb_smtpad` elements with `shape: "polygon"`.

This change adds proper polygon padstack generation during Circuit JSON -> DSN conversion and ensures image pin coordinates are derived from the polygon center, preventing `NaN` values in exported DSN output.

## What Changed

- Added polygon SMT pad geometry extraction helper
- Added polygon padstack creation for DSN library output
- Updated padstack naming to support custom polygon pad descriptors
- Updated image pin generation to use polygon-derived center coordinates
- Added a repro test covering polygon SMT pad export

## Bug

Polygon SMT pads were being routed through rectangle/circle-oriented export logic. Because polygon pads do not provide `x`, `y`, `width`, or `height` in the same way as other SMT pad shapes, DSN export could emit invalid values such as `NaN` in padstack and pin coordinate data.

## Fix

Polygon pads are now handled explicitly during export:

- polygon points are normalized
- polygon bounds and center are computed from point data
- DSN polygon padstacks are emitted with finite coordinate lists
- image pins use the computed polygon center instead of missing direct pad coordinates

## Test Coverage

Added a repro test that:

- constructs a Circuit JSON component with a polygon SMT pad
- exports it to DSN JSON and DSN string
- verifies the DSN output does not contain `NaN`
- verifies exported polygon padstack coordinates are finite
- verifies exported pin coordinates are finite

## Scope

This PR fixes polygon SMT pad **export** correctness for Circuit JSON -> DSN conversion.

Round-trip preservation of polygon SMT pad geometry during DSN -> Circuit JSON import is still not implemented and can be handled separately.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/repros`

title: Fix missing schematic_table symbol conversion with regression coverage
description: Fixes missing schematic_table and schematic_table_cell support in generateSymbolTsx, ensuring table-based schematic symbols survive conversion and are properly represented in the generated JSX tree.
body:
## Summary
- fix missing `schematic_table` and `schematic_table_cell` support in `generateSymbolTsx`
- reconstruct `<schematictable>`, `<schematicrow>`, and `<schematiccell>` output from circuit-json table data
- extend `test7` so the missing table conversion is reproduced and covered in the same regression fixture
- spread the schematic fixture elements apart so the SVG snapshot stays readable

## Why
`schematic_table` data already exists in circuit-json, but the symbol converter ignored it entirely. That meant table content was dropped during circuit-json -> tscircuit symbol conversion, even though the upstream schematic primitives already support tables.

This change fixes that gap by mapping table position, border width, cell padding, row heights, text, alignment, font size, and row/column spans into the generated JSX tree.

## Impact
- table-based schematic symbols now survive conversion instead of disappearing
- the regression is covered in the same test fixture that exercises schematic symbol generation
- the SVG snapshot is easier to inspect because the fixture elements no longer overlap

## Validation
- `bun test tests/test7-support-schematic-elements.test.tsx`

title: Preserve polygon and rounded SMT pad geometry in DSN imports with round-trip regression coverage
description: Fixes a regression in DSN import where polygon-based SMT padstacks lose their geometry when converted back to circuit-json, ensuring accurate rendering and fidelity during round-trips.
body:
## Summary

This PR fixes a DSN import regression where polygon-based SMT padstacks could lose their intended geometry when converted back to circuit-json.

The main bug was that custom polygon pads and rounded polygon padstacks were not being classified consistently during `dsn -> circuit-json`, which caused visual regressions and broke round-trip fidelity.

## Why this matters

This is a reproduce-and-fix regression PR focused on core PCB data modeling.

It improves shape fidelity during DSN round-trips by preserving:
- custom polygon SMT pads as polygon pads
- rounded polygon padstacks as polygon geometry instead of flattening them into plain rectangles
- true rectangular polygon padstacks as rectangular pads when that shape can be recovered safely

That means imported boards render more accurately and downstream session/snapshot tests reflect the real pad geometry.

## What changed

- fixed `dsn -> circuit-json` SMT padstack conversion logic for polygon-based pads
- preserved custom polygon SMT pads as `shape: "polygon"` with point-based geometry
- avoided flattening rounded polygon padstacks into plain rectangular pads
- kept rectangular polygon padstacks importing as `rect` when their geometry is actually rectangular
- updated the DSN session test to validate trace alignment against stable `pcb_port` coordinates instead of assuming every imported SMT pad exposes `x/y`
- added and updated regression coverage for:
  - polygon SMT pad export
  - `circuit-json -> dsn -> circuit-json` polygon round-tripping
  - DSN session / visual snapshot behavior

## Impact

This PR combines:
- a bug reproduction and fix
- subtle but important geometry/rendering corrections
- improved circuit-json shape fidelity during DSN import

In practice, it fixes visual regressions in imported boards and makes DSN round-trips much more trustworthy for non-rectangular SMT pads.

## Verification

- `bun test tests/repros/repro16-polygon-smtpad-export.test.ts`
- `bun test tests/dsn-pcb/circuit-json-to-dsn-session.test.ts`
- `bun test tests/dsn-pcb/parse-dsn-json-to-circuit-json.test.ts`
- `bun test tests/repros/repro7-smoothie-board.test.ts`

title: Replace Buffer-based GLTF renderer with Uint8Array API in repro02 snapshot test
description: Replaces Buffer-based GLTF rendering with Uint8Array API in repro02 snapshot test, updating snapshots accordingly.
body:
## Summary

- Bumps `poppygl` from `^0.0.17` to `^0.0.24`
- Replaces `renderGLTFToPNGBufferFromGLBBuffer` (returns Node.js `Buffer`) with `renderGLTFToPNGFromGLB` (returns `Uint8Array`) in the `repro02` snapshot test
- Updates snapshots to reflect the new render output

## What changed

`poppygl` now exports `renderGLTFToPNGFromGLB` as the preferred rendering function. It has the same options signature as the old `renderGLTFToPNGBufferFromGLBBuffer` but returns a `Uint8Array` instead of a Node.js `Buffer`, making it runtime-agnostic (works in Bun, browsers, and edge runtimes without polyfills).

## Why

`Buffer` is Node.js-specific. Using `Uint8Array` aligns with the modern, runtime-portable API surface and keeps the codebase compatible with non-Node runtimes like Bun (which this project already uses for tests).
